summary: Gruber reviews Apple TV's new fanfare (sonic logo/mnemonic), composed by Finneas O'Connell and created with agency TBWA Media Arts Lab. After watching it across several Apple TV series over a few weeks, he declares he loves it. He argues the old piano-chord fanfare, while a neat callback to the Macintosh startup chime, never reached the iconic status of Netflix's 'tudum' or HBO's static-and-om. The new version succeeds both sonically and visually, with its rotating prism effect evoking the classic six-color Apple logo. He notes the prism was created practically with real glass rather than CGI, and praises how the new branding makes Apple TV content feel more rooted in Apple's heritage rather than a passing Hollywood experiment.
key_points: The old Apple TV fanfare evoked the Macintosh startup chime — a neat idea that never achieved iconic status alongside Netflix's 'tudum' or HBO's static
The new fanfare, composed by Finneas, succeeds as a standalone branding element that sounds rich and distinctive without evoking anything else
The rotating prism visual, shot practically with real glass, evokes the classic six-color Apple logo, connecting Apple TV more deeply to Apple's heritage
The rebrand helps Apple TV original content feel less like a possible passing fancy and more like an integral part of Apple
Finneas designed the mnemonic to withstand binge-watching repetition — 'the bite of ginger between rolls'
money_quotes: The old one was not bad. But 'not bad' should never be good enough for Apple.
What makes that chord great for a computer booting up doesn't make it great for a cinematic sonic logo.
Netflix's 'tudum' is so iconic that it's the name of their company blog.
The new one feels like a branding stroke unto itself. Sonically, it doesn't evoke anything else. It just sounds rich and cool.
Despite moving away from a sonic callback to the Macintosh, the overall effect feels more rooted to Apple's on-the-cusp-of-a-half-century history.
The change makes Apple TV original content feel more like a part of Apple, less like a possible passing fancy.
It matters not to me that they photographed this practically, rather than via computer-generated graphics, but the bottom line is that it looks cool, timeless, and Apple-y.
key_insight: Apple TV's new fanfare succeeds where the old one fell short by creating a distinctive sonic and visual identity that roots the streaming service in Apple's heritage rather than merely referencing it.
